narrative: As a white male applicant with an upper-middle-class family background, your law school application was evaluated using a predictive model that assesses the likelihood of passing the bar exam on the first try, based on profiles from thousands of past students. The model estimated a 40% chance that you would fail the bar exam, which crossed the threshold for rejection—meaning the university sees this as too high a risk for admission. This decision hinged on just a few key aspects of your profile, with your LSAT score, Year 3 school decile ranking, and family income standing out as the strongest influences. Your LSAT score of 40, which is above the typical student's 36.7, was the most powerful positive factor, strongly pushing the prediction toward you passing the bar by signaling solid analytical skills. However, your Year 3 decile of 1—far below the average of 5.53—had an even stronger counteracting effect, pulling heavily toward a failure prediction because it highlights relatively weaker performance in your later undergraduate years compared to peers. Your upper-middle family income also helped somewhat, nudging the odds in favor of passing, as students from similar brackets tend to have more resources supporting their success. While your undergraduate GPA of 2.6 is below the average 3.21 and your Year 1 decile of 2 trails the typical 5.71, these played smaller roles here, and your plan to attend full-time aligns with the vast majority of students. Overall, your profile shows strengths like that standout LSAT but is weighed down by those lower decile rankings, placing you below the bar for students typically predicted to pass with high confidence. This isn't a judgment on your potential as a white male from a supportive background—many in similar situations succeed with targeted improvements. To move forward, focus on bolstering your academic record, perhaps through a post-baccalaureate program or additional coursework to lift those decile-like metrics, and consider retaking the LSAT if it can go even higher. Reapplying after demonstrating stronger late-stage performance could shift the model's view dramatically. You're clearly capable, given that LSAT edge—keep building on it.
